item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great example of portraying the Foundation as a competent, scientific organisation. This scip could have been about three paragraphs shorter and would have left me asking questions like "But what if they used hormone treatments?". Instead it answered all my questions about containment while also leaving me wondering what the hell "The King" actually is. A fantastic scip +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. ████: Can you describe to me why you feel this way?

SCP-1284-1/7: You are denying my king his right.

Dr. ████: How so?

SCP-1284-1/7: We were meant to consummate our marriage, but you prevented it.

Dr. ████: And how did we do that?

SCP-1284-1/7: Your pills.

Dr. ████: If we didn't give you those pills, you would bleed to death.

SCP-1284-1/7: It is his right.

Anthony Michaels would like to say hello.

item.title: Chilling creepy done right
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to morhadow for pointing this article to me.

It's been a long while since I've felt really creeped out by anything. This did it. This did it all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Delightfully disturbing piece, the "Moon King" really sells itself as an eldritch, elusive figure, along the lines of the Scarlet King himself. +1

Also, there are a lot of bunny-related moon skips, aren't there? I never really noticed before.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

During the interview, SCP-1284-1/1 makes reference to a reality-bending rabbit king on the moon, of sorts. There are several other rabbit/moon anomalies which would be referenced following this; as well as another reality-bender located on the moon that could also be referenced.

Proposing adding this immediately after the interview:

Addendum: ██/██/████: Research into a connection between SCP-1284 and other leporine anomalies associated with the moon, such as SCP-1282 and SCP-1640, is ongoing. A connection between "the king" mentioned by SCP-1284-1/1 and SCP-2686 has been ruled out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably my favorite part is that the Foundation actually partially figured out how the "King" entity was defeating their hormone treatments and developed an effective mundane countermeasure. It's nice to see the Foundation actually being competent and outsmarting an anomalous being with a realistic scientific strategy, rather than just stumbling on some seemingly random esoteric containment ritual that works but they have no idea why.

item.title: Re: It is his right.
item.content:encoded: I don't think I would have known that if I didn't read chat. the evidence is there, but it didn't immediately stick out to me when I read the article. you sir, are a genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Menarche has been invariably fatal to instances of SCP-1284-1, resulting in continuous, rapid hemorrhaging of the endometrium until death by exsanguination. […] SCP-1284-1 instances indicate awareness that this will occur, with expressed feelings about it varying from slight nervousness to eager anticipation.

This was my oh shit moment. Hooked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually describing their reactions during the event would be crossing a line I'm not personally comfortable crossing in my article.

…Ew ew ew ew ew ew ew ew ew
Point taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Coincidentally, I actually only discovered the Hidden Verses last night. :P

I'm glad that the number of SCP-1284-1 came out to eight instead of seven (originally it was going to be 18, but didn't really have anything interesting to put for the 10 in-between), because otherwise that would have been very awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some impressions are immediate, and some connections only come later, when you least expect them. I am now quite disturbed by the similarities in the King/Bride motif and other little things between this and the Hidden Verses in SCP-231.

item.title: Re: Science scone
item.content:encoded:
^This. The issue isn't that there isn't energy being put into the system by digestion; there is. There just isn't enough of it to account for what her body is actually doing with that mass. She doesn't gain or burn nearly enough calories nearly fast enough for the regeneration to be possible on her own power. She has all the bricks, mortar, and other materials you need to build a church, but only one mason, who is nonetheless somehow getting that church built as well and fast as if he were an entire construction team, and our best explanation so far is that maybe he's being helped by ghostly construction workers who spontaneously generated out of the aether, because we have no idea where all that extra work came from.

EDIT: You know what? I'm just gonna change this detail to something that's still anomalous, but easier for me to describe and still in the spirit of what I was going for (Sepcifically, that while she has all the materials she needs, she is definitely skipping a step).

item.title: Re: Science scone
item.content:encoded: The way I understand it, if you have a kg of rabbit meat in your stomach, and you want to grow a new limb from it, you'll have to expend energy. The brides grow limbs with 100% efficiency; all the mass of the limbs comes from the rabbit meat, but where do they get the energy for the regeneration no one knows.

item.title: Science scone
item.content:encoded:
I love the detail and how it all hangs together. Just one quibble: You wrote "limb regeneration has not been observed to violate conservation of mass, but does appear to violate conservation of energy"

These laws are normally identical - if you add energy, you add mass. I wondered if maybe you meant that mass was conserved, but entropy wasn't (energy was being redistributed in improbable ways)? But, I'm not sure exactly how energy isn't being conserved - the flesh-rabbits went out and ate other things, right? So it's not a closed system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are absolutely correct. That is indeed what is happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 from me. The bit at the end about the Foundation getting the hormone treatment to work after randomised blood testing was a nice detail: it sounds to me like whatever power is responsible for 1284 was deliberately adjusting the girls' hormone levels so they would look normal at the regular times the samples were originally taken, to prevent the Foundation from developing a working treatment, but it was stymied (perhaps only temporarily) when they switched to random testing, giving them a better idea of her actual condition so they could adjust the treatment to work properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this based on the Japanese moon rabbit folktale?

Only in the extremely loose sense of having chosen rabbits because they have an association with the moon in some cultures.

How does it do this? Does it have claws that it uses to rip pieces of itself off? Does it liquify itself from the inside-out and regurgitate itself into the girl's open mouth?

I pictured it flaking off bite-sized portions of itself, but that's probably not as scary as what someone else could think of.

It'd make things that much creepier of the girls showed happiness/ecstasy while bleeding to death.

Actually describing their reactions during the event would be crossing a line I'm not personally comfortable crossing in my article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this based on the Japanese moon rabbit folktale?

One out of every 8 instances of SCP-1284-2 formed in this way will attempt to path its way back to SCP-1284-1 and feed itself piecemeal to SCP-1284-1.

How does it do this? Does it have claws that it uses to rip pieces of itself off? Does it liquify itself from the inside-out and regurgitate itself into the girl's open mouth?

Menarche has been invariably fatal to instances of SCP-1284-1, resulting in continuous, rapid hemorrhaging of the endometrium until death by exsanguination.

We were meant to consummate our marriage, but you prevented it.

It'd make things that much creepier of the girls showed happiness/ecstasy while bleeding to death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps "formation event"?

Edit: Actually, yes, I like "formation event". Shall be using that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must admit I had to re-read this a few times but I think I do like it. Very weird and very different. My one bugbear is the uses of 'spawn event', it just isn't clinical enough for me when people use it. Also when used in an article it always makes me lose some of my immersion and joy in reading it. Personally I think it would read better as something like "the selective autotomizing of limbs resulting in instances of SCP-XXXX-2" (autotomize is the term used for lizards dropping their tails) but maybe this is a little confusing and verbose. I am not really too picky what you call it, just make it accurate at not a ‘spawn event’.
That being said, have my +1. My bugbears are not enough to make me no vote.

Edit: miscopied the word autotomize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent in all the right places. I have a soft spot for originally-done monsters, and -2s are what made me upvote. And then the interviews were so good that I would've upvoted just for them. And all the little details just make this shine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. I also really liked the first two footnotes. They nicely show the Foundation being thorough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the detail you put into this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried to cut it down, but all the info given would be important in-universe for people reading it to know. I can't just [DATA EXPUNGED FOR BEING BORING TO READ]. Well, I could, but I don't think it'd be a good idea. Yes, heavily abridging the info about the nature and behaviour of -2 would get you to the out-of-universe payoff faster because it's not actually all that important to the story, but in-universe the part that wants to gnaw your feet off is the most important thing to know about in detail.

(e: Also, articles that lack answers/implication of answers to obvious, easily-testable questions bug me. They've been containing this one for literally decades, so I expect there to be oodles and gobs of data on the things that can be tested or examined relatively easily. I've relegated the vast majority of it to the realm of not-included supplemental files, but what I've got sans the addenda is the minimum of what I would expect of the in-universe main file.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, I love unseen recurring entities

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've actually been working on this one for a long while in my sandbox. Close timing and numbering are both coincidental.

I went for the technical-but-highly-precise words in large part because the article itself is already pretty long. Are there any specific phrases or sentences you'd like to see clarified?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two skips in a row that involve people turning into rabbits and vice-versa beneath the light of the moon. Is there something in the water?

This really gets better as it goes. The narrative formed by the interviews is strong. I found the initial paragraphs of the description nearly indecipherable, however, and would recommend another look at those.

Also, seriously, wtf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding that last part, but I like the rest to let it slide. Hesitant upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well. That was unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have intentionally left out all of their related supplement files, in part because I find supplement files to be a pain in the ass to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1/8 has a second psychosis on top of the one that comes with being a 1284-1. -1/3 and -1/7 are both exhibiting the standard 1284-1 psychosis, in which those events that should be distressing to a normal person are not; they're fully aware of what's going to happen to them, but they don't see anything wrong with the arrangement. -1/8's additional psychosis makes it so things that even a 1284-1 finds distressing get retconned out of her internal narrative. So -1/8, for example, doesn't ever remember getting her blood drawn, because needles hurt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because it doesn't happen in two different subjects.

SCP-1284-1/8 suffers from an additional psychosis similar to Depersonalisation Disorder without successful reality-testing, ultimately forming a personal narrative in which unpleasant events and stimuli are derealised entirely.

Compare to the interviews:

Dr. ████: If we didn't give you those pills, you would bleed to death.

SCP-1284-1/7: It is his right.

SCP-1284-1/7 is totally aware of what should happen and eventually kills herself because of it. That's not derealising something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job, this takes what is basically "creepy girl makes monsters and then they eat you" into an interesting story with enough mystery at the different reading points to keep attention engaged. I'm assuming it's intentional, but incident 1284-1/05-009 is referenced in the text and then nothing more is made of it. It seems like it could be developed further down in the log when the reader reaches instance 5 if you wanted another point of interest. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, the amount of detail given makes it work that much more for me - the article is believable as a document, and at every stage there was this "Oh, so they thought of this method, too. And it didn't work" which is a feeling I love in articles - in short , portraying the Foundation as competent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written and a very interesting idea. I think some of the paragraphs are quite lengthy and can sometimes take away from the overall sense of the scp. But besides that, Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do you say the misperception of reality is not normal, then show that it happens in two different subjects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea here, but I think its buried under the article. I founds its length disproportionate to the ideas it presented. I would've loved to have an "ooooh" moment, but instead it was more of an "oh."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took a few things thematically from folklore (e.g., rabbits are associated with the moon in many places, another term for menstrual blood is "moon blood"), but the king and his brides are not based on any actual real-world myth.

item.title: It is his right.
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
The 'king' is real.

The girls are not the true anomaly. They're the victims of it. Or, rather, him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good and you should feel good.

I have the feeling this is an SCP take on some myth, but I can't think of which one. If you made it up completely, you should feel even better.
